question: Define a mixed autoregressive-moving average process, ARMA(p,q), and write its general equation.
expectedPoints: An ARMA(p,q) process is a combination of an Autoregressive (AR) component of order p and a Moving Average (MA) component of order q.
The general model is expressed as: X_t = c + φ₁X_{t-1} + ... + φ_pX_{t-p} + ε_t + θ₁ε_{t-1} + ... + θ_qε_{t-q}
Where ε_t is a white noise process, φ are the AR parameters, and θ are the MA parameters.

question: Explain the principle of parsimony in the context of ARMA modeling and discuss one practical advantage it provides.
expectedPoints: Parsimony is the principle of using the simplest model (i.e., the one with the fewest parameters) that adequately explains the data.
In ARMA modeling, this means finding values for p and q that are as low as possible while still capturing the essential dynamics of the time series.
A key advantage is that more parsimonious models tend to provide better forecasts because they have fewer parameters to estimate, which reduces forecasting error and the risk of overfitting the data.

question: Compare and contrast pure AR, pure MA, and mixed ARMA processes. Your answer should include their key characteristics, how they are identified using ACF and PACF plots, and a discussion on why an analyst might choose an ARMA model over a pure model.
expectedPoints: Key Characteristics: A pure AR(p) process has only a 'memory' of its own past values. A pure MA(q) process has only a 'memory' of past shocks. An ARMA(p,q) process combines both, having memory of both past values and past shocks.

Identification: The ACF of an AR(p) process tails off, while its PACF cuts off after lag p. The ACF of an MA(q) process cuts off after lag q, while its PACF tails off. The ACF and PACF of an ARMA process both tail off, making them harder to distinguish from each other.

Model Choice: An analyst chooses an ARMA model when the data-generating process is a mixture of both AR and MA components. This often leads to a more parsimonious model (e.g., an ARMA(1,1)) than a high-order pure AR or MA model would require to fit the same data, resulting in a more efficient and better forecasting model.
